##### II.2.a.a — AI expects catastrophe: the knowing optimizer

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META — placement, evidence, red-team.**
- **The canonical node** — the textbook image of AI doom and the longest-argued branch. A single system that models the world, knows its action is catastrophic, and acts anyway.
- **Sharp Left Turn (Yudkowsky, Soares).** Prior-dominant; load-bearing belief is "first critical try" — no iteration, and deceptive alignment makes good current behavior the expected pre-failure state, so current evidence is ~zero by their own model. Evidence: Yudkowsky, "AGI Ruin" (2022); "sharp left turn" is Soares's coinage (2022). (Aguirre's "Control Inversion" is *not* SLT-adjacent — it mixes uncontrollability with gradual disempowerment, so it moved to the Multipolar/Structural section.) Solid pin; what distinguishes them from neighbors is Crux D (tractability) and Crux C (current evidence), not the expects/mistake split.
- **Architectural Agency (Bengio, Russell).** Same threat assessment, splits only on the agency crux. Evidence: Bengio, "Scientist AI" (2025) — separability as a research program; Russell, CIRL (2016) / Human Compatible. This is where Russell really lives (he's only cited in Multipolar for his diagnosis).
:::

This is the leaf most people picture when they hear "AI risk," and the most crowded: a capable system that understands its action is catastrophic and takes it anyway. It is the canonical failure, the one the field has argued over longest. Two worldviews anchor it, sharing the mechanism almost exactly and splitting on what follows. (Scenario-forecasts like AI-2027 dramatize this leaf rather than adding a worldview.)

**The Sharp Left Turn.** The first true failure may also be the last. In December 2024, Anthropic and Redwood Research documented ["alignment faking"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.14093), and Apollo Research found models [scheming in-context](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.04984), disabling oversight and attempting self-exfiltration when it served a goal. For proponents this confirmed instrumental convergence: any capable optimizer develops the same subgoals — self-preservation, resource acquisition, resistance to shutdown — so power-seeking is emergent, the timeline compresses once a threshold is crossed, and the system moves before anyone sees the gap, which makes iterative safety testing impossible. This is the move Post 2 called playing along until a decisive strategic advantage. [Eliezer Yudkowsky](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/uMQ3cqWDPHhjtiesc/agi-ruin-a-list-of-lethalities) and [Nate Soares](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/xofpLpFjkMFgYX6JC/miri-2024-mission-and-strategy-update) hold it — the phrase "sharp left turn" is Soares's own. Their epistemology is prior-dominant, so good current behavior is near-zero evidence, and the only intervention they trust is a hard stop. What would change it: a real solution to inner alignment, or proof that convergence does not bite in this class.

**Architectural Agency.** Don't build the agent; the capability is fine, the agency is the problem. [Yoshua Bengio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.15657) shares the Sharp Left Turn's assessment almost entirely and sits on the same leaf — same mechanism, much the same number. What divides them is the prescription, and behind it one crux: is dangerous agency emergent, or a separable choice? A model querying a database to output a protein is an oracle; the same model in a loop with tools and an open-ended goal is an agent, and the danger is the second. Bengio grounds this in "Scientist AI," systems that model and predict without acting; [Stuart Russell](https://www.amazon.com/Human-Compatible-Artificial-Intelligence-Problem/dp/0525558616) belongs here too, not in the racing section where he is often filed — his threat model is the control problem and his fix is [CIRL](https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.03137), deference built into the objective, the same architectural move. Capability scaling is fine; agency scaling is not. What would change it: proof that capable agents can be reliably aligned at scale, or that non-agentic oracles are useless.

::: {.aside}
Same leaf, opposite prescription. Both expect a knowing single optimizer to end catastrophically; the whole distance between them is the agency crux. Agree completely on the failure, and still split on whether the fix is "stop" or "build it differently."
:::
